2: The Transfiguration's mountain has traditionally been identified as Mount Tabor (in Galilee) or Mount Hermon (on the northern border). The six-day interval (17:1) after Peter's confession echoes the six days before Moses's ascent of Sinai (Exod 24:16). Peter's suggestion to build three booths (skenai) reflects the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot), the festival of dwelling in booths, which in Second Temple Judaism had strong eschatological associations — the time when YHWH would dwell with his people in the messianic era. Peter instinctively reaches for the Sukkot-framework to interpret the theophanic dwelling-in-glory he is witnessing.
1: "After six days" (meth’ hēmeras hex) — the six-day interval between the Caesarea Philippi confession and the Transfiguration echoes Exod 24:16: "The glory of the LORD dwelt on Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days; on the seventh day he called to Moses from within the cloud." The Transfiguration is structurally framed as a Sinai theophany — a divine self-disclosure on a mountain, preceded by a waiting period. Peter, James, and John were the inner circle of the Twelve, the three witnesses of Jesus’ most intimate moments (cf. 26:37’s Gethsemane).
3: Moses and Elijah (Mōysēs kai Ēlias) appearing with Jesus represented the two primary divisions of the OT as a whole — the Law (Moses) and the Prophets (Elijah as the paradigmatic prophet) — and also the two figures expected to appear at the eschaton. Malachi 4:4-5 ended the OT by invoking both "the teaching of my servant Moses" and "the prophet Elijah" who would come before the Day of the LORD. Their appearance together identifies the Transfiguration as an eschatological scene and places Jesus in continuity and climactic superiority to both.
4: "Lord, it is good that we are here. If you wish, I will make three tents here, one for you and one for Moses and one for Elijah" — Peter’s offer to build three skēnas (booths/tents) reflects the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) framework. Sukkot was celebrated by building temporary booths (sukkot) in memory of the wilderness dwelling and in anticipation of the eschatological dwelling of God with his people (Zech 14:16-19; John 7:2 sets the first "I am" discourse near Sukkot). Peter instinctively reaches for the Sukkot-framework to interpret the theophanic dwelling-in-glory he is witnessing.
5: The bright cloud (nephelē phōteinē) overshadowing the disciples is the shekinah — the visible divine presence-cloud that appeared on Sinai (Exod 24:15-18), guided Israel in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22), filled the Tabernacle (Exod 40:34-38), and filled Solomon’s Temple at its dedication (1 Kgs 8:10-11). In Second Temple Judaism, the departure of the shekinah from the Temple (Ezek 10-11) was associated with the Babylonian exile; its eschatological return was eagerly anticipated. The voice from the cloud — "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased; listen to him" — echoes the baptismal voice (3:17) with the addition of "listen to him," activating Deut 18:15 (Moses’ prophecy of the coming prophet: "to him you shall listen").
6: The disciples falling on their faces (epesan epi prosōpon autōn kai ephobēthēsan sphodra) is the standard OT theophany response: prostration before divine presence (Gen 17:3; Ezek 1:28; 43:3; Dan 8:17; 10:9). "Were terrified greatly" (ephobēthēsan sphodra) activates the sacred fear that distinguishes genuine theophany encounters from ordinary religious experience in the OT tradition. The disciples are not merely awed by a spectacular sight — they are responding to an actual encounter with divine presence.
7: "But Jesus came and touched them, saying, 'Rise, and have no fear'" — the divine touch that resolves theophanic fear is a recurring pattern in Daniel (8:18; 10:10, 16, 18) where the figure who appears touches the fallen prophet and commands him to stand. Jesus’ action places him in the role of the divine comforter who lifts the prostrate from theophanic overwhelm. The command "have no fear" (mē phobeisthe) is the standard divine reassurance after theophany encounters (Gen 15:1; 26:24; Dan 10:12).
8: "And when they lifted up their eyes, they saw no one but Jesus only" (Iēsoun monon) — the disappearance of Moses and Elijah leaves Jesus as the single figure remaining. This is the Transfiguration’s theological conclusion: the Law and the Prophets have been witnesses, but Jesus is the one. The voice’s command "listen to him" (v. 5) is made visually concrete: Moses and Elijah are gone; only Jesus remains to be heard. The narrative enacts the "fulfillment" claim of 5:17 ("I have not come to abolish but to fulfill").
9: The command to "tell no one the vision until the Son of Man is raised from the dead" restricts the Transfiguration account to the post-Resurrection period. The Transfiguration as a vision (to horama = what was seen, a technical term for prophetic vision) would be incomprehensible without the Resurrection: a transfigured Jesus who was then crucified would produce misunderstanding about the nature of his mission. The Resurrection reframes the Transfiguration as a proleptic disclosure of resurrection glory rather than an escape from suffering.
10: "Why do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?" — the disciples’ question connects the Transfiguration appearance of Elijah with the scribal tradition derived from Mal 4:5-6 (Elijah comes before the Day of the LORD). If Elijah has appeared at the Transfiguration, does this fulfill the scribal expectation? The disciples are trying to map the traditional eschatological sequence onto what they have just witnessed. The question reflects genuine theological puzzlement rather than doubt.
11: "Elijah does come, and he will restore all things" — Jesus acknowledges the validity of the scribal interpretation of Malachi: Elijah’s coming to "restore all things" (apokatastēsei panta) is legitimate eschatological expectation. The technical term apokatastasis (restoration, reconstitution) was used in Jewish apocalyptic for the eschatological restoration of Israel and the world to its proper order. Jesus affirms the expectation before reinterpreting its fulfillment.
12: "But I tell you that Elijah has already come, and they did not recognize him, but did to him whatever they pleased. So also the Son of Man will suffer at their hands" — the identification of John the Baptist as the fulfillment of the Elijah expectation (cf. 11:14) is paired with the Passion prediction: John’s treatment (imprisonment and beheading by Herod) anticipates Jesus’ treatment. The "they did to him whatever they pleased" echoes the fate of the prophets in the deuteronomistic tradition (2 Chr 36:16; Jer 26:20-23; Neh 9:26): Israel consistently rejected and killed its prophets.
13: "Then the disciples understood that he was speaking to them of John the Baptist" — the disciples’ comprehension of the John-as-Elijah identification comes here, after the Transfiguration, whereas earlier (11:14) it required interpretive "willingness." The Transfiguration’s disclosure of Jesus’ glory has apparently opened the disciples’ capacity to understand the eschatological reframings Jesus has been offering.
14: The epileptic boy (selēniazomenos = moon-struck) represents the ancient medical/popular belief that certain conditions (convulsions, mental illness, epilepsy) were related to lunar cycles. The term "lunatic" in English derives from luna (moon) via this ancient tradition. Josephus (Ant. 8.2.5) describes Solomonic-tradition Jewish exorcism techniques; the attribution of convulsive conditions to demonic agency was the standard ancient etiology. The father’s detailed description (falls into fire and water, frequent episodes) suggests genuine epilepsy or a severe convulsive disorder.
15: The father’s approach with prostration (gonypetōn auton = kneeling before him) follows the same posture of petition seen throughout Matthew’s healing narratives (8:2; 9:18; 15:25). The request "have mercy on my son" (eleēson mou ton huion) is the elemental petition form — mercy as the ground of appeal rather than merit. The detail that the disciples could not cure him creates the tension resolved by Jesus’ exorcism-healing.
16: The disciples’ failure to heal is presented without explanation at this point, building the tension that the private explanation in vv. 19-20 resolves. The failure is not attributed to lack of willingness or of authority — Jesus gave them authority over unclean spirits in 10:1 — but will be attributed to faith-inadequacy. The public failure of the disciples in front of a crowd was a social embarrassment that the private inquiry (v. 19: "came to Jesus privately") addressed.
17: "O faithless and twisted generation (genea apistos kai diestrammenē), how long am I to be with you?" — the address echoes Moses’ exasperated lament in Deut 32:5 ("a crooked and twisted generation") and 32:20 ("for they are a perverse generation, children in whom is no faithfulness"). The wilderness generation’s faithlessness provides the prophetic framework for the present generation’s failure. The question "how long am I to be with you?" expresses the weariness of the divine patient presence with human recalcitrance, not personal frustration.
18: The exorcism by Jesus’ command (kai epetimēsen autō̄ ho Iēsous) is immediate and complete, contrasting with the disciples’ failure. The rebuke (epitimaō) is the standard exorcism command verb in the Synoptics (8:26; 17:18; Mark 1:25; 9:25; Luke 4:35). The boy’s instantaneous healing ("and the boy was healed instantly") publicly restores what the disciples’ failure had left broken, vindicating Jesus’ authority while highlighting the disciples’ limitation.
19: The disciples’ private question "Why could we not cast it out?" reflects their awareness of the gap between their authority (given in 10:1) and their actual performance. The private inquiry (coming to Jesus "privately," kat’ idian) is characteristic of the disciples’ learning pattern in Matthew: public witness followed by private explanation. They are asking about methodology and capacity, not about whether the mission remains valid.
20: "Because of your little faith (di’ oligopistian hymōn)" — oligopistia (little faith) is Matthew’s characteristic term for the disciples’ condition (6:30; 8:26; 14:31; 16:8). The mustard seed comparison — "if you have faith like a grain of mustard seed, you will say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it will move" — is a Jewish hyperbole for impossibly great action. The Talmud (b. Berakhot 64a; b. Sanhedrin 24a) uses "uprooter of mountains" as a title for exceptional sages; "nothing will be impossible for you" is the promise’s scope, not a claim that physical mountains literally move on command.
21: Verse 21 — "But this kind never comes out except by prayer and fasting" — is absent from the best early manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and was likely added from Mark 9:29. This is a textual variant where the shorter reading is almost certainly original. The verse reflects the early church’s practice of fasting before exorcism, which is attested in Didache 7:4 (fasting before baptism) and other early Christian sources. Its addition to Matthew by later scribes reflects the incorporation of fasting as a standard spiritual discipline into the apostolic commission narrative.
22: "As they were gathering in Galilee" — the second Passion prediction occurs during Jesus’ continued Galilean ministry, before the final journey to Jerusalem (19:1). The geographical note situates the prediction at the heart of his earlier ministry region, creating a contrast between Galilee (where his ministry flourished among the people) and Jerusalem (where death awaited). The repetition of the Passion prediction (first at 16:21, now 17:22-23) reinforces its centrality and ensures the disciples have heard it before the events occur.
23: "They will kill him, and he will be raised on the third day (tē̄ tritē̄ hēmera̧ egerthēsetai)" — the passive voice "will be raised" (egerthēsetai) is the consistent NT form for resurrection: God raises Jesus rather than Jesus self-resurrecting. The "third day" formula derives from Hos 6:2 ("after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up") and may also reflect the Jewish belief that the soul hovered near the body for three days before final departure (m. Yebamoth 16:3). "They were greatly distressed" (elypēthēsan sphodra) — the disciples’ grief is recorded despite or alongside their comprehension; hearing and receiving are different responses.
24: The Temple tax collection scene occurs in Capernaum — Jesus’ home base. The two-drachma tax (to didrachmon) was the annual half-shekel contribution required of all adult male Jews for the Temple’s maintenance (Exod 30:13-16; 38:26), equivalent to two days’ wages. This tax was collected annually, typically in the month of Adar (February-March) before Passover, through collection booths set up throughout Jewish communities including the Diaspora. By Capernaum the tax-collectors were local Jewish agents of the Temple, not Roman tax farmers.
25: Peter’s immediate "Yes" to the collectors and Jesus’ preemptive question ("What do you think, Simon?") before Peter can report indicates Jesus’ knowledge of the encounter. The analogy — "From whom do kings of the earth take toll or tax? From their sons or from others?" — employs common practice: rulers tax their subjects, not their own household. The "sons" who are exempt are the ruling family’s members; the "others" are the taxed subjects. Applied to the Temple: if Jesus is the Son of the Temple’s God, he is exempt from the Temple tax by the same logic.
26: "Then the sons are free" (eleutheroi ara eisin hoi huioi) — the freedom of the king’s children from tax is both the logical conclusion of the analogy and a statement about Jesus’ identity. As the Son of the living God (16:16), Jesus is the "son" in relation to the Temple (the house of his Father); the Temple tax is paid to support the Father’s house, but the Son of the house is not taxed to support his own Father’s household. The exemption is theological, not a claim to civil immunity from taxation.
27: "However, not to give offense (hina de mē skandalisōmen autous)" — the decision to pay the tax despite the theological exemption is an application of the community principle: freedom exists but should not be exercised when it causes unnecessary stumbling in others. Paul applies the same principle in 1 Cor 8-9 (freedom to eat idol-meat, restricted by neighbor’s conscience). The miracle of the coin in the fish’s mouth provides the tax payment: a coin (statēr) worth exactly four drachmas = two didrachma taxes for two people (Jesus and Peter), paid miraculously from the sea that Jesus had just walked on and calmed.
